title: Hormuz Road Map Signed, Mines Still Blocking Normal Traffic; Russia Imports Indian Gasoline as Refineries Crumble
subhead: US-Iran talks in Switzerland produced a demining coordination mechanism but 80 mines still close the main shipping lane, keeping Hormuz traffic at roughly 20% of pre-war levels. Ukraine's drone campaign has pushed Russian refining to a two-decade low, forcing Moscow to subsidize Indian gasoline imports.

sections.heading: Hormuz: Road Map Signed, but the Main Channel Remains Closed
sections.body:
The first direct US-Iran negotiations since 1979 concluded Monday at the Bürgenstock Resort on Lake Lucerne, Switzerland. Vice President JD Vance led the American delegation; Iran's Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf led the Iranian side. Mediators from Qatar and Pakistan described the outcome as 'encouraging progress' and a 'road map' toward a final settlement.

The talks produced three concrete outcomes: a joint statement declaring 'encouraging progress,' a commitment to begin technical-level talks immediately, and — critically for shipping — a coordination mechanism for mine clearance in the Strait of Hormuz. Vance also announced that Iran agreed to invite IAEA inspectors back into the country, calling it 'a major milestone' toward denuclearization.

The physical reality on the water, however, remains far from normal. Approximately 80 naval mines laid by Iran during the conflict still block the Traffic Separation Scheme — the standard inbound and outbound lanes that have governed commercial navigation since 1968. According to Intertanko marine director Phil Belcher, 'the main route through the middle of the strait of Hormuz, that's closed, that's dangerous.' The US Navy's Joint Maritime Information Center has issued an advisory directing all vessels to avoid the standard lanes. Ships are instead being routed along a narrower corridor near Iran's Larak Island — what Belcher compared to 'using the hard shoulder' of a highway where the main road is closed.

Naval mine clearance doctrine holds that clearing a minefield takes up to 200 times longer than laying one. The Pentagon's internal estimate places full minesweeping at up to six months using three dedicated vessels already in the region, according to TechTimes reporting.
sections.whyItMatters: The diplomatic architecture is moving faster than the physical one. Even with a demining coordination mechanism agreed, Hormuz won't return to pre-war throughput (130+ ships/day) until the standard shipping lanes are certified clear — a process measured in months, not weeks.

sections.heading: Hormuz Traffic: Numbers That Matter
sections.body:
The gap between 'legally open' and 'commercially normal' is vast. Before the conflict, roughly 120-140 vessels transited the strait daily, carrying about 20% of global oil supply and 25% of seaborne oil trade. On June 18 — the first full day after the MOU took effect — AXS Marine data recorded 25 commercial crossings, the highest single-day count since April 18 but still less than 21% of pre-war normal.

Polymarket's IMF Portwatch-linked tracker found commercial transits still running at 5-10% of pre-February levels, with fewer than 10 vessels daily. US Energy Secretary Chris Wright claimed on Fox News that 67 ships transited on a single day and that volumes were 'roughly comparable to pre-conflict levels' — a claim that requires heavy context: pre-war daily traffic averaged 120-140 ships, and the higher single-day figures represent a backlog release of laden vessels exiting the Gulf, not a durable step-up in inbound traffic.

Kpler, the crude analytics firm, estimated that recovery to roughly 50% of pre-war throughput (~40 transits/day) was achievable within 30 days if no setbacks emerged. The IRGC's warning shots at vessels near the strait on June 19 — and the cancellation of scheduled follow-up US-Iran talks in Switzerland that same day — represent exactly the kind of setback Kpler's analysts were modeling around.

Approximately 550-600 vessels remain stranded in the Gulf, including 160 tankers, 200 bulk carriers, and 60 container ships, according to Lloyd's List and TechTimes. About 20,000 seafarers have been stuck on either side of the channel. War-risk insurance premiums remain elevated.
sections.whyItMatters: Optimistic statements about 'reopening' mask a physical bottleneck that will take weeks to months to clear. The 80 mines, IRGC permit requirements, signal jamming, and insurance uncertainty mean pre-war volumes won't return until late 2026 at the earliest — per Lloyd's List editor Richard Meade: 'I don't think shipping in the strait is getting back to normal this year.'

sections.heading: Oil Prices Steady After Post-Deal Plunge, But Recovery Ceiling Looms
sections.body:
Brent crude fell to $82/barrel on June 15 — a three-month low — after President Trump announced the US-Iran MOU, and dropped a further 5% the following day as deal details emerged. Prices have since stabilized but remain well below the $126/barrel peak reached during the height of the Iran conflict, and above the $69/barrel 2025 average.

Capital Economics chief economist Neil Shearing estimated that roughly 80% of pre-crisis crude flows could resume by end of Q3 2026, but that LNG and other non-crude exports face a longer tail. SEB chief commodities analyst Bjarne Schieldrop noted that a gradual Hormuz reopening is tactically preferable for Iran as well — it prevents governments from restocking emergency crude too quickly and preserves Tehran's leverage during nuclear negotiations.

The IEA had warned in late May that oil markets were nearing a 'red zone' where summer demand would collide with fast-depleting stockpiles. The deal pulled the market back from that edge, but the slow physical clearance means inventory draws will continue for weeks.
sections.whyItMatters: The price relief is real but incomplete. With mines still in the channel and 600 vessels queued, the market is pricing in a phased recovery rather than a snapback. The $80-90/bbl range likely persists through Q3 as restocking demand meets constrained throughput.

sections.heading: Russia's Refining Crisis Deepens: Indian Gasoline, Lower Standards, Aviation Fuel Squeeze
sections.body:
Ukraine's drone campaign against Russian refineries has pushed domestic crude processing to its lowest level in two decades, cutting gasoline production by roughly 25%. According to Bloomberg and KyivPost, 16 refinery facilities were struck in May alone — the highest monthly total since the full-scale invasion began — and at least 6 more in June.

The most consequential recent strike hit Gazprom Neft's Moscow refinery in the Kapotnya district, which normally supplies up to 40% of Moscow's fuel. The attack targeted the AVT-6 distillation unit, potentially crippling the facility's entire 12 million ton/year capacity. Forbes described it as a 'landmark in complex drone strikes,' noting the coordinated use of multiple drone types including the AN-196 Lyutyi, Fire Point FP-1, Morok, and possibly the new Sichen delta-wing drone — the first confirmed combat use.

The cumulative damage has opened a structural shortfall: Russian refineries produce approximately 85,000 tons of gasoline per day against summer demand of 111,000 tons — a gap of roughly 25,000 tons daily, or about 20% of domestic consumption per Reuters. Wholesale gasoline prices have topped 100 rubles (~$1.34) per liter. Major retailers — Rosneft, Lukoil, Tatneft — have imposed purchase limits at stations across Moscow, St. Petersburg, and Tatarstan.

Russia has responded with a familiar playbook: lowering fuel quality standards indefinitely, extending what was supposed to be a temporary relaxation of Euro-5 norms (gasoline sulfur now allowed at 150 mg/kg vs. the 10 mg/kg Euro-5 limit, effectively reverting to Euro-3 levels per Kommersant via KyivPost). The gasoline export ban runs through July 31; diesel and aviation fuel exports are also restricted.

Most strikingly, Moscow is preparing to import gasoline from India on a large scale. According to RBC, draft amendments to Russia's Tax Code would introduce budget subsidies for oil companies importing gasoline from Indian ports, with subsidies calculated based on Indian market prices plus shipping costs. The bill has backing from the Duma's budget committee and could pass as early as this week. India — which became Russia's largest crude buyer at 2.66 million barrels/day in June 2026 — now looks set to sell back refined product to its supplier.

At least 25 Russian regions are now experiencing fuel supply disruptions, up from 15 in early June, including Crimea and occupied Sevastopol. Aviation fuel is also tightening: light aircraft operators have begun substituting automobile gasoline, and wholesale aviation fuel prices have surged despite the export ban.
sections.whyItMatters: The drone campaign has exposed a structural vulnerability: Russia can pump crude but increasingly cannot turn it into usable fuel. The spectacle of the world's second-largest oil exporter subsidizing imports of refined product from its own crude customer — India — marks a remarkable inversion. Lowered quality standards and regional shortages will worsen through summer.

sections.heading: G7 Tightens Russia Sanctions as Falling Oil Price Removes the Revenue Shield
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Évian-les-Bains on June 16, leaders agreed to increase pressure on Russia 'notably through sanctions on oil and gas,' with President Zelensky in the room for the session.

Britain moved immediately, designating over 20 additional shadow-fleet tankers and a GRU procurement network it named 'Neptune' — three companies and ten GRU officers accused of covertly acquiring Western technology for the Russian armed forces, with named third-country suppliers in China, Thailand, and Turkey. The UK also intercepted a banned oil tanker.

Canada followed with its own expansion: 120 additional shadow-fleet vessels designated, along with seven individuals and over 30 entities targeting energy revenues, defense-industrial actors, and shipping participants. Notably, the Canadian designations reached beyond Russian entities to include a Vietnamese ship owner (Hung Phat Maritime Trading), a New Zealand-based insurer (Maritime Mutual Insurance Association), and UAE-based ship managers (Novashipmanagement, Citrine Marine).

The timing is sharp. As Grosswald.org noted, the Iran ceasefire pulled Brent to its lowest since early March, and the price shield that cushioned Russian oil revenue through three years of sanctions is coming off. Cheaper oil widens the gap between Russia's war spending and its receipts. The Neptune designation also signals a maturing campaign — targeting the procurement network that buys Western components for Russian weapons, attacking the supply chain rather than just the revenue stream.

The test, as Grosswald observed, is enforcement against third-country suppliers in China, Thailand, and Turkey — 'where designation is easy and interdiction is not.'
sections.whyItMatters: Sanctions and the oil price are finally pulling in the same direction. With Brent below $85 and falling, Russia's fiscal cushion narrows precisely as the sanctions architecture expands from cargo interdiction to capability disruption. But enforcement against third-country intermediaries remains the weak link.

sections.heading: Iran's 60-Day Waiver and the Nuclear Negotiations Ahead
sections.body:
Alongside the Switzerland road map, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent announced a temporary general license authorizing the production, delivery, and sale of Iranian crude oil through August 21, 2026 — a 60-day oil sanctions waiver designed to give Tehran an economic incentive to keep Hormuz open while formal nuclear negotiations proceed.

The MOU signed at Versailles on June 17 reportedly includes: an agreement to end hostilities, reopen the strait for mine removal, and a 60-day negotiation window over the terms of Iran's nuclear phaseout. Iran's agreement to readmit IAEA inspectors was described by Vance as 'the first step in permanently denuclearizing' Iran's weapons program.

The diplomacy remains fragile. Iran's IRGC declared the strait closed on June 21, citing continued Israel-Hezbollah fighting in Lebanon as a breach of the MOU's requirement that hostilities end on all fronts. The US military disputed the closure, stating that 55 commercial vessels transited that day carrying over 17 million barrels. The fundamental legal disagreement over transit rights under UNCLOS remains unresolved by the MOU.

President Trump also posted a social-media threat during the Switzerland session, warning that the US would 'hit Iran very hard again' if Iran's proxy forces in Lebanon continued fighting — a reminder that the deal sits alongside military pressure, not apart from it.
sections.whyItMatters: The 60-day waiver is both carrot and clock. It gives Tehran real revenue and the US real leverage — if nuclear talks stall, the waiver expires and Iran's oil flows freeze again. But the IRGC's unilateral declaration that Hormuz is closed, and Israel-Hezbollah fighting that continues regardless of the MOU, show how fragile the arrangement remains.

sections.heading: IRGC Permit Regime and Insurance: The Hidden Barriers to Normal Shipping
sections.body:
Even if all 80 mines were cleared tomorrow, commercial shipping through Hormuz still faces two non-physical barriers that no diplomatic text can remove by decree.

First, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps has established a transit permit system for vessels passing through the strait. According to TechTimes, shippers must obtain IRGC authorization before transit — a mechanism that gives Tehran de facto gatekeeping power over commercial traffic regardless of the MOU's terms. On June 19, the IRGC fired warning shots at vessels near the strait and ordered a Hong Kong-flagged tanker and a Saint Kitts and Nevis-flagged bulk carrier to turn back.

Second, war-risk insurance premiums remain elevated, and the insurance market has not yet recalibrated to the MOU. As the Guardian reported, Lloyd's List and Intertanko have both cautioned that underwriters need demonstrable, sustained safe passage before rates return to pre-conflict levels. Signal jamming — electronic interference with ships' GPS and navigation systems, reportedly carried out by Iran during the conflict — adds another layer of risk for insurers.

The combination means that even the ships exiting the Gulf now are largely those that have been stranded for 100+ days and are simply leaving, not a resumption of normal two-way trade. About 160 tankers have been trapped in the Gulf since February, and the one-way exodus of laden vessels is the easy part — getting empty tankers back in to load is a different calculus entirely when IRGC permits, mines, and insurance costs all create friction.
sections.whyItMatters: The MOU opened the legal door, but the IRGC permit regime, mine clearance timeline, and insurance market together constitute the real gate. Normal shipping requires all three to align — and none is close to resolved. The 5-10% transit levels will climb slowly, not snap back.

bottomLine: The Switzerland road map is a genuine diplomatic step — first direct US-Iran talks since 1979, IAEA inspectors invited back, a demining coordination mechanism agreed — but the Strait of Hormuz remains physically blocked by 80 mines, commercially constrained by IRGC transit permits, and financially hampered by war-risk insurance costs. Normal traffic won't resume for months. Meanwhile, Russia's refining sector is in freefall: Ukraine's drone campaign has cut output to a two-decade low, forcing Moscow to subsidize Indian gasoline imports and lower domestic fuel quality standards indefinitely. The G7's expansion of shadow-fleet and procurement sanctions hits at a moment when falling oil prices have removed the revenue cushion that insulated Russia's war budget — but enforcement against third-country intermediaries in China, Thailand, and Turkey remains the open question.
sources: The Guardian, TechTimes, CNBC, OilPrice, Forbes, KyivPost, Grosswald.org, Baker McKenzie, Al Jazeera, NPR, Lloyd's List, Kpler, SEB, Capital Economics, Kommersant (via KyivPost), RBC (via KyivPost)
